coredata.dc:title: A dual-functional monomer-based molecularly imprinted fluorescent aptasensor employing near-infrared carbon dots for selective detection of quinine in food
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xuyuan
Liu, Hao
Dai, Zhengyuan
Wang, Ying
Li, Li
Ding, Yaping
coredata.dc:description: A molecularly imprinted fluorescent aptasensor was designed for selective detection of quinine (Qn) based on dual functional monomers. In the sol-gel polymerization of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and quinine aptamer (Apt) were employed as dual functional monomers, and Qn was the template molecule. Near-infrared carbon dots (RCDs) were used as fluorescence signal probe, and effectively avoided the interference of fluorescence emitted by Qn. Due to the diverse interaction among APTES, Apt and Qn, the near-infrared fluorescent molecularly imprinted aptasensor (RCDs-Apt@MIPs) exhibited higher specificity towards Qn than the sensor with APTES or Apt as a single functional monomer. The sensor had a wide linear range of 5.0–110.0 μM with a low detection limit of 1.7 μM. Satisfactory recovery between 93.4% and 104.0% with relative standard deviation from 1.30% to 3.60% in drinks, which showed great potential that this method can be used to detect Qn in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Aptamer
Fluorescence
Quinine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Covalent linking of RCDs to Apt
Fluorescence determination of Qn by RCDs-Apt@MIPs
Selectivity, repeatability and stability of RCDs-Apt@MIPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274096).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of RCDs, (B) Fluorescence spectra of RCDs and RCDs-Apt@MIPs.
(A) FT-IR spectrum of RCDs, (B) XPS spectrum of RCDs, (C) FT-IR spectrum of RCDs-Apt@MIPs and (D) XPS spectrum of RCDs-Apt@MIPs.
(A) Effect of elution times, (B) Effect of pH value, (C) Effect of RCDs-Apt@MIPs concentration and (D) Effect of impact on response time.

The fluorescence spectra of (A) RCDs-Apt@MIPs, (C) MIPs-Apt and (E) MIPs-APTES to detect different concentrations of Qn. Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity value of (B) RCDs-Apt@MIPs, (D) MIPs-Apt and (F) MIPs-APTES and different concentrations of Qn.
(A) UV–vis spectra of Qn, RCDs, Qn + RCDs and emission spectra of RCDs, (B) Emission of RCDs under different excitation.
(A) The selectivity of RCDs-Apt@MIPs, (B) The selectivity of RCDs-Apt@NIPs, (C) The repeatability of RCDs-Apt@MIPs and (D) The stability of RCDs-Apt@MIPs.
The synthesis mechanism diagram (A) RCDs and RCDs-Apt, (B) RCDs-Apt@MIPs.
Comparison with other reported methods for the detection of Qn.
The recovery of RCDs-Apt@MIPs in drinks sample.

head.ce:title: A dual-functional monomer-based molecularly imprinted fluorescent aptasensor employing near-infrared carbon dots for selective detection of quinine in food

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A molecularly imprinted fluorescent aptasensor was designed for selective detection of quinine (Qn) based on dual functional monomers. In the sol-gel polymerization of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and quinine aptamer (Apt) were employed as dual functional monomers, and Qn was the template molecule. Near-infrared carbon dots (RCDs) were used as fluorescence signal probe, and effectively avoided the interference of fluorescence emitted by Qn. Due to the diverse interaction among APTES, Apt and Qn, the near-infrared fluorescent molecularly imprinted aptasensor (RCDs-Apt@MIPs) exhibited higher specificity towards Qn than the sensor with APTES or Apt as a single functional monomer. The sensor had a wide linear range of 5.0–110.0 μM with a low detection limit of 1.7 μM. Satisfactory recovery between 93.4% and 104.0% with relative standard deviation from 1.30% to 3.60% in drinks, which showed great potential that this method can be used to detect Qn in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quinine (Qn) is a naturally occurring alkaloid commonly found in the bark of cinchona trees grown in South America. Qn has been used to treat malaria for more than three hundred years. (Buleandra et al., 2018; Dar et al., 2012) It inhibits the synthesis of parasite DNA by forming complexes with nucleic acids (Kluska et al., 2016). In addition to being used as a medicine, Qn is a very popular food additive which is commonly added in non-alcoholic carbonated beverages as a flavoring and bittering agent (Rottmann et al., 2010). However, Qn is a potentially toxic drug. Generally, the plasma concentration of Qn is in the range of 3–7 μg·mL−1, and levels exceeding 10 μg·mL−1 may lead to adverse reactions (Samanidou et al., 2005; Zaugg and Thormann, 2001), such as dizziness, decreased vision, and even renal failure and cardiac dysrhythmias (Huston and Levinson, 2006). Currently, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) limits the concentration of Qn in carbonated drinks to 83 mg·L−1, and the concentration of Qn in other nutritional drinks is in the range of 25–60 mg·L−1 (Tzanavaras et al., 2013). Greek legislation stipulates that the maximum concentration of Qn in non-alcoholic beverages is 100 mg·L−1, and 45 mg·L−1 in beverages containing fruit juice (Wolf et al., 1992). Therefore, it is important to develop a sensitive and rapid sensor for the determination of Qn.

Researchers have designed many analytical methods to determine Qn in food. It mainly includes atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Yebra and Cespón, 2000), capillary electrophoresis (Mikuš et al., 2012), electrochemistry (Geto et al., 2012), chemiluminescence (Liu et al., 2022), liquid chromatography (Jansson et al., 2003) and mass spectrometry (Shrivas and Wu, 2007), etc. However, these methods have the disadvantages of polluting the environment, time-consuming, requiring professional and technical personnel, and expensive equipment.

Fluorescent sensors based on carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention in recent years (Cho and Park, 2019). Especially near-infrared carbon dots (RCDs) show the excellent optical properties, low toxicity and easy preparation (Shao et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2019). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have three-dimensional cavities that can specifically recognize template molecules (Ren and Fan, 2023). Díaz-Liñán et al. proposed a molecularly imprinted paper-based analytical device (MIP PAD) and achieved the detection of Qn in soda drink samples (Díaz-Liñán et al., 2019). However, MIPs lack of signal transduction ability. To address the limited signal transduction capability of molecularly imprinted polymers, a fluorescence molecular imprinted sensor was developed by integrating RCDs with molecular imprinting technology (Tang et al., 2022). In addition, aptamer (Apt) is also a widely used biological recognition factor (Nuntawong et al., 2024). They are short single-stranded DNA or RNA screened from nucleic acid libraries by the systematic evolution of ligands through exponential enrichment (SELEX) (Lyu, Khan and Wang, 2021; Tuerk and Gold, 1990). Apt can be folded into different spatial conformations to bind with the target molecule (Fang and Tan, 2010; Orava et al., 2010), which can be used as functional monomers in MIPs (Huang et al., 2023). This binding force between the molecules gives the Apt high affinity and specificity. In addition, Apt has the characteristics of small size, stable chemical properties, low price, and easy modification (Zhou and Rossi, 2017; Guo et al., 2024). The combination of MIPs and Apt can take advantage of their respective advantages to make up for their inherent shortcomings and improve the detection performance of the sensor (Geng et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2022). Zhao et al. constructed a biosensing method based on fluorescent covalent organic frameworks (COFs) with Apt and MIPs for the detection of diethylstilbestrol (DES), and it was applied to detect fish samples (Zhao et al., 2024). Duan et al. designed a paper-based fluorescent sensor based on metal-organic framework and molecularly imprinted polymers for the detection of malachite green (Duan et al., 2023). Nevertheless, there are few studies on fluorescent molecularly imprinted aptamer sensors using RCDs, and the blue CDs synthesized under normal circumstances cannot avoid the blue fluorescence emitted by Qn.

Herein, a near-infrared fluorescent molecularly imprinted aptasensor (RCDs-Apt@MIPs) was designed for the determination of Qn. APTES was used as the chemical functional monomer, Apt as the biological functional monomer, and RCDs as the fluorescence signal transduction, achieving sensitive detection of Qn. The combination of dual functional monomers with molecularly imprinted polymers offers the dual benefits of sensitivity and selectivity. Therefore, the near-infrared carbon dots fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor with Apt and APTES as bifunctional monomers shows strong potential for development in food analysis and sensing.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was conducted on Phenom ProX G6 (Phenom Scientific Co., Ltd., China). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed on JEM 2100F (Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan). Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra was required on AVATAR370 (Thermo Nicolet Corporation, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on ESCA LAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific China Co., Ltd., China). Fluorescence spectra were collected by using the F-4700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-3600iPlus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Japan).

The detailed sequence of quinine aptamer was 5′-GGCGACATGGAAAATCCTTCAACGAAGTGGGTCGCC-3′ (Bennett et al., 2023) and quinine aptamer was synthesized by Tsingke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99%), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 99%), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS, 97%), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 98%), citric acid (99%), ethanol (99%), acetic acid (99%), quinine (Qn, 98%), urea (99%), MgAc2 (99%), MnAc2 (98%), CuAc2 (99%), CoAc2 (99%), ammonia (28%) and NaOH (96%) were purchased by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 28%), creatinine (99%), glucose (99%), uric acid (99%), NaHCO3 (99%), AgNO3 (99%), KH2PO4 (99%) and K2HPO4 (99%) were obtained by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents used in the experiments above were of analytical grade.

The synthesis of RCDs was based on the previously reported solvothermal method (Zhang et al., 2020). 8.03 wt% of citric acid and 16.06 wt% of urea were dissolved in 75.91 wt% of DMF and sonicated for 5 min to dissolve the solids completely. The mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting mixture was centrifuged. Each 2 mL of supernatant was added with 4 mL of 50 mg·mL−1 NaOH and centrifuged for purification. The obtained RCDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent linking of RCDs to Apt
ce:section.ce:para: The amide reaction was utilized to link the RCDs to the Apt by covalent bonding. To 300 μL of RCDs, 500 μL of EDC (1.0 mg·mL−1) was added as an activator and 500 μL of NHS (0.5 mg·mL−1) was added as a linker, and the mixture was stirred for 1 h at room temperature and protected from light. RCDs-aptamer (RCDs-Apt) was obtained by stirring 100 μL aptamer with 300 μL of the above mixed solution in the dark for 12 h.

2 mg of Qn was dissolved in 2 mL of ethanol, 400 μL of the above RCDs-Apt was added, and the pre-polymer was obtained by stirring for 30 min away from light. Then, 50 μL of APTES was added and stirred for 30 min away from light. Then 200 μL of TEOS was added as a cross-linking agent, 100 μL of ammonia was added as an initiator, and 2 mL of deionized water was finally added. The above mixture was magnetically stirred for 12 h to obtain the polymer. Then, the template molecules were eluted with ethanol and acetic acid (v/v = 9:1) for 5 times to obtain RCDs-Apt@MIPs. Finally, RCDs-Apt@MIPs was dried at 60 °C for 12 h and stored at 4 °C away from light.

For comparison, the non-molecularly imprinted polymers (RCDs-Apt@NIPs) were synthesized using the same method, but the addition of the template molecules Qn was omitted during the synthesis. Using the same method, only APTES or Apt was added in the imprinting process to prepare single functional monomer polymers, which were APTES as functional monomer (MIPs-APTES) and Apt as functional monomer (MIPs-Apt).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of Qn by RCDs-Apt@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the standard curve, 4 mg of RCDs-Apt@MIPs was weighed and dispersed in 10 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and sonicated for 5 min to remove air bubbles. Different concentrations of Qn (5.0–110.0 μM) were added to 0.4 mg·mL−1 of RCDs-Apt@MIPs solution in the appropriate order. After 15 min of reaction, the fluorescence spectrometer with a detection slit of 10 nm/10 nm and a detection wavelength between 600 and 800 nm was used at the temperature of 30 °C.

The synthesis process for RCDs-Apt@MIPs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The synthesis strategy can be divided into five parts: (1) Citric acid and urea were used as carbon and nitrogen sources to prepare RCDs via the hydrothermal method. (2) RCDs-Apt was obtained by covalently linking RCDs with Apt. (3) The pre-polymer was obtained by mixing RCDs-Apt with Qn. (4) Apt and APTES were polymerized to form the molecularly imprinted polymers. (5) Eluents were used to elute template molecules in order to obtain specific cavities, and finally RCDs-Apt@MIPs was obtained.

In Fig. 1A, it is evident that RCDs-Apt@MIPs exhibits noticeable voids, while Fig. 1B demonstrates that RCDs-Apt@NIPs microspheres are densely packed without any voids. It can be inferred that there is a distinct gap between RCDs-Apt@MIPs and RCDs-Apt@NIPs after elution. The TEM images of RCDs are presented in Fig. 2A, which illustrates that the RCDs are spherical with a size of about 1–3 nm and evenly distributed. As depicted in Fig. 2B, when excited at 560 nm, the fluorescence of RCDs-Apt@MIPs is slightly weaker than that of RCDs. However, both RCDs-Apt@MIPs and RCDs exhibit the maximum emission peak at 668 nm.

Meanwhile, the functional groups on the surface of RCDs and RCDs-Apt@MIPs were identified using FT-IR spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 3A, the characteristic band of CO is observed at 1632 cm−1 for RCDs, while the range of 3050–3650 cm−1 corresponds to CN absorption, and peaks at 1498 cm−1 are attributed to OH and NH stretching vibrations, with a methylene tensile vibration observed at 2975 cm−1. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 3C, the FT-IR spectra of RCDs-Apt@MIPs reveal characteristic bands at 1064, 785 and 450 cm−1 corresponding to SiOSi and SiO bonds respectively, along with a peak at 1550 cm−1 indicating NH stretching vibration, confirming the formation of a silicon layer on the RCDs (Wu and Tong, 2020). Furthermore, compared to RCDs alone, RCDs-Apt@MIPs exhibits additional characteristic peaks for SiOSi and SiO bonds. The results from FT-IR spectrum analysis confirm that RCDs-Apt@MIPs has been successfully prepared.

RCDs and RCDs-Apt@MIPs were characterized by XPS, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3B and Fig. 3 D. The XPS spectra of RCDs (Fig. 3B) have the following characteristic peaks: CN/CO (287 eV), pyrrole N (398 eV), CO (532.4 eV) (Pan et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2023). In the XPS spectrum of RCDs-Apt@MIPs (Fig. 3D), 279 eV corresponds to the characteristic peak of C 1s. 400 eV corresponds to N 1s spectral peak and the spectral peak of O 1s is 536 eV. And Fig. 3D shows that there is an absorption peak of Si 2p at 104 eV (Xie et al., 2023). The successful preparation of RCDs-Apt@MIPs was further confirmed.

The frequency of elution, the dose of RCDs-Apt@MIPs, pH and reaction time were optimized with fluorescence intensity as the index. As shown in Fig. 4A, when elution reached 5 times, the presence of Qn could hardly be detected by UV–vis spectrophotometer, and there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity, so the elution times were chosen to be 5 times. In this study, the dosage of RCDs-Apt@MIPs is also optimized. As shown in Fig. 4C, RCDs-Apt@MIPs has the optimal adsorption effect when the concentration of RCDs-Apt@MIPs is 0.4 mg·mL−1. PBS buffer solution with different pH (pH 5.5, 6.5, 7.5, 8.5, 9.5, 10.5) was prepared. RCDs-Apt@MIPs powder was added to the buffer solution to prepare 0.4 mg·mL−1 RCDs-Apt@MIPs solution. The fluorescence spectra of RCDs-Apt@MIPs at different pH were scanned. As shown in Fig. 4B, the corresponding F-F0 is the largest when the pH is 7.5, and pH 7.5 is adopted as the test condition in this experiment. As shown in Fig. 4D, when the reaction time is 15 min, the fluorescence intensity changes most obviously, so the reaction time is 15 min.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, 0.4 mg·mL−1 RCDs-Apt@MIPs was used to detect different concentrations of Qn. In Fig. 5A, the fluorescence spectra showed that the fluorescence intensity of RCDs-Apt@MIPs increases with the increase of Qn concentration. In Fig. 5B, when the concentration of Qn was 5.0–110.0 μM, the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship. The corresponding linear equation is y = 8.00x + 370.29, y = F, where F is the fluorescence intensity after adding the detection substance Qn, x is the concentration of Qn, and LOD is 1.7 μM (S/N = 3). As shown in Fig. 5C and Fig. 5E, when there was only one functional monomer, the detection of Qn by fluorescent probe was not obvious. In Fig. S1, RCDs-Apt@NIPs cannot distinguish Qn in the linear concentration range.
Compared with other similar work, as shown in Table 1 , the molecularly imprinted fluorescent aptasensor prepared in this work has a wider linear range and a lower detection limit for Qn.

As shown in Fig. 6A, it can be seen from the UV–vis spectrum of the RCDs that the ultraviolet absorption at 280 nm and 320 nm belongs to the n-π* transition inside the RCDs (Li et al., 2023). A strong emission enhancement known as aggregation-induced emission (AIE) is reported upon aggregation induced by the oppositely charged co-component (Kaur et al., 2024). The hydrogen bond formed between the negatively charged carboxyl group and the protonated Qn on the RCDs surface and the larger π conjugation system inhibited the rotation of the RCDs and enhanced the radiation transition. Therefore, the fluorescence response mechanism can be attributed to AIE. When Qn was added, there was no significant change in the UV–vis absorption intensity at 280 nm and 320 nm, but the UV–vis absorption intensity was weakened at 560 nm, which can be inferred that there is an interaction between the RCDs and Qn. The mechanism of fluorescence enhancement of Qn can be attributed to the AIE between RCDs and Qn (Hu et al., 2024). The emission wavelength of RCDs was tested under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 6B, the optimal excitation wavelength is 560 nm. From Fig. 6A, it can be obtained that RCDs have the UV–vis absorption at 560 nm, therefore, it is feasible to use 560 nm as the fluorescence excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability and stability of RCDs-Apt@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: To detect the selectivity of RCDs-Apt@MIPs, other interfering substances were selected: Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Na+, Ag+, HCO3 −, citric acid, creatinine, urea, glucose, uric acid. When the concentration of interfering substance is 100 times that of Qn, the fluorescence intensity of RCDs-Apt@MIPs and RCDs-Apt@NIPs has no significant change, as shown in Fig. 7A and Fig. 7B. Therefore, the sensor has a high selectivity, indicating a good repeatability of the prepared sensor.

In Fig. 7C, the detection effect of the same RCDs-Apt@MIPs remains good after 6 elution detection cycles, indicating a good repeatability of the prepared sensor. The fluorescence intensity of RCDs-Apt@MIPs was measured daily to characterize its stability. Fig. 7D shows that the fluorescence intensity of RCDs-Apt@MIPs has no significant change after 50 days, which indicates that the prepared sensor has a good stability.

To verify the accuracy and effectiveness of the prepared sensor, Qn was tested in drinks sample. The drinks used in the experiment were bought from Shanghai University Education Supermarket. First, the drinks were centrifuged for 5 min and ultrasound treated for 15 min. The supernatant was collected and filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. Qn in drinks was detected using the RCDs-Apt@MIPs with a concentration of 0.4 mg·mL−1. In order to explore the feasibility of sensor detection in real samples, the calibration method was used to study RCDs-Apt@MIPs. As shown in Table 2 , the standard recovery rate was 93.40%–104.00%. The satisfactory recovery indicates that RCDs-Apt@MIPs can be applied to the actual detection of Qn in drinks sample.

In summary, a molecularly imprinted fluorescent aptasensor based on RCDs and bifunctional monomers was proposed in this work, and it was applied to the detection of Qn in actual drinks sample. The RCDs were used as fluorescence signal to avoid the interference of Qn self-fluorescence. Using APTES and Apt as functional monomers, the detection sensitivity of RCDs-Apt@MIPs to Qn was improved. This work holds great significance for the detection and analysis of Qn in food.

Xuyuan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hao Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhengyuan Dai: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Ying Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Li Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yaping Ding: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274096).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.142317.
